name: Mock Test - 06
instruction: The Question Paper contains three sections. Section A has 25 questions. Attempt any 20 questions. Section B has 24 questions. Attempt any20 questions. Section C has 6 questions. Attempt any 5 questions. All questions carry equal marks. There is no negative marking.

questions.question_text: A charge Q is divided into two parts of q and Q-q. If the coulomb repulsion between them when they are separated is to be maximum, the ratio of {tex}\frac{Q}{q}{/tex} should be
questions.question_html: A charge Q is divided into two parts of q and Q-q. If the coulomb repulsion between them when they are separated is to be maximum, the ratio of {tex}\frac{Q}{q}{/tex} should be
questions.explanation: F = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q(Q-q)}{x^{2}}{/tex} x is constant. For maximum force, {tex}\frac{d F}{d q}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d}{d q}{/tex} (qQ - q 2 ) = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Q - 2q = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{Q}{ q}{/tex} = 2

questions.question_text: Submarine cables act as
questions.question_html: Submarine cables act as

options.text: cylindrical capacitor with outer cylinder earthed
options.html: cylindrical capacitor with outer cylinder earthed

options.text: cylindrical capacitor with inner cylinder earthed
options.html: cylindrical capacitor with inner cylinder earthed

questions.correct_option_text: cylindrical capacitor with outer cylinder earthed
questions.explanation: A submarine cable consists of an inner conductor which carries power. This conductor is covered by an insulator, which acts as a dielectric. The dielectric material is covered by a metal coating called shield, which is connected to ground. The cable acts as a cylindrical capacitor, with the conductor acting as the inner cylinder, and the metal shield as the outer cylinder which is connected to earth.

questions.question_text: A 6 V battery is connected to the terminals of a three-meter long wire of uniform thickness and resistance of 100 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . The difference of potential between two points on the wire separated by a distance of 50 cm will be:
questions.question_html: A 6 V battery is connected to the terminals of a three-meter long wire of uniform thickness and resistance of 100 {tex}\Omega{/tex}. The difference of potential between two points on the wire separated by a distance of 50 cm will be:
questions.explanation: In a potentiometer, V {tex}\propto{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{V_{2}}{V_{1}}=\frac{l_{2}}{4}{/tex} or {tex}V_{2}=\frac{l_{2}}{l_{1}} \cdot V_{1}=\frac{0 \cdot 50}{3} \times 6{/tex} = 1 V

questions.question_text: The point charges Q and -2Q are placed some distance apart. If the electric field at the location of Q is E, then the electric field at the location of -2Q will be:
questions.question_html: The point charges Q and -2Q are placed some distance apart. If the electric field at the location of Q is E, then the electric field at the location of -2Q will be:
questions.explanation: Electric field of -2Q at the location of charge Q, {tex}\kappa \frac{(-2 Q)}{r^{2}}{/tex} = E Electric field of Q at the location of -2Q, E' = {tex}\kappa \frac{Q}{r^{2}}=-\frac{E}{2}{/tex}

questions.question_text: If the electric current in a lamp decreases by 5%, then the power output decreases by
questions.question_html: If the electric current in a lamp decreases by 5%, then the power output decreases by
questions.explanation: Power, P = I 2 R {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{{P_2}}}{{{P_1}}} = {\left[ {\frac{{{I_2}}}{{{I_1}}}} \right]^2}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{{P_2}}}{{{P_1}}} = \left[ {\frac{{0.95 \times 0.95{I^2}}}{{{I^2}}}} \right] = 0.9025{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Decrease in power {tex} = \left( {1 - \frac{{{P_2}}}{{{P_1}}}} \right) \times 100{/tex} Power decrease {tex} \approx {/tex} 10%

questions.question_text: Kirchhoff's first law, i.e., {tex}\Sigma{/tex} I = 0 at a junction, deals with the conservation of
questions.question_html: Kirchhoff's first law, i.e., {tex}\Sigma{/tex}I = 0 at a junction, deals with the conservation of
questions.explanation: Charge

questions.question_text: Which of the following quantities remain constant in a step down transformer?
questions.question_html: Which of the following quantities remain constant in a step down transformer?
questions.explanation: Energy losses be zero in transformers hence power remains constant in step down and step up transformer also.

questions.question_text: Relative permittivity and permeability of a material are {tex}\varepsilon_r{/tex} and {tex}\mu_r{/tex} respectively. Which of the following values of these quantities are allowed for a diamagnetic material?
questions.question_html: Relative permittivity and permeability of a material are {tex}\varepsilon_r{/tex} and {tex}\mu_r{/tex} respectively. Which of the following values of these quantities are allowed for a diamagnetic material?
questions.explanation: For a diamagnetic material, relative permittivity is greater then 1 and relative permeability is less than 1.

questions.question_text: An AC generator consists of
questions.question_html: An AC generator consists of

options.text: A permanent magnet rotor and a stationary coil.
options.html: A permanent magnet rotor and a stationary coil.

options.text: Two stationary coils close to each other
options.html: Two stationary coils close to each other

options.text: A stationary coil for emf and a movable coil fed with AC
options.html: A stationary coil for emf and a movable coil fed with AC

options.text: A coil that can rotate in a permanent magnetic field and is provided with slip rings
options.html: A coil that can rotate in a permanent magnetic field and is provided with slip rings

questions.correct_option_text: A coil that can rotate in a permanent magnetic field and is provided with slip rings
questions.explanation: AC generators operate on the same fundamental principles of electromagnetic induction. The turning of a coil in a magnetic field produces motional emfs in both sides of the coil. A slip ring is a method of making an electrical connection through a rotating assembly. Formally, it is an electric transmission device that allows energy flow between two electrical rotating parts, such as in a motor.

questions.question_text: An LC circuit contains a 20 mH inductor and a 50 {tex}\mu {\rm{F}}{/tex} capacitor with an initial charge of 10 mC. The resistance of the circuit is negligible. Natural frequency of the circuit is
questions.question_html: An LC circuit contains a 20 mH inductor and a 50 {tex}\mu {\rm{F}}{/tex} capacitor with an initial charge of 10 mC. The resistance of the circuit is negligible. Natural frequency of the circuit is
questions.explanation: {tex}L = 20mH = 20 \times {10^{ - 3}}H{/tex} {tex}C = 50\mu F = 50 \times {10^{ - 6}}F{/tex} Frequency, {tex}f = \frac{1}{{2\pi \sqrt {LC} }}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{{2 \times 3.14\sqrt {20 \times {{10}^{ - 3}} \times 50 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}} }}{/tex} = 159 Hz

questions.question_text: The magnetic field at a distance r from a long wire carrying current I is 0.4 tesla. The magnetic field at a distance 2r is:
questions.question_html: The magnetic field at a distance r from a long wire carrying current I is 0.4 tesla. The magnetic field at a distance 2r is:
questions.explanation: {tex}\frac{B_{2}}{B_{1}}=\frac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}=\frac{r}{2 r}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore B_{2}=\frac{1}{2} B_{1}=\frac{1}{2} \times {/tex} 0.4 = 0.2 T

questions.question_text: The current I in the given circuit is:
questions.question_html: The current I in the given circuit is:
questions.explanation: The two cells are connected oppositely. Total emf = 5 - 2 = 3 V Total resistance = 10 + 20 = 30 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Current = {tex}\frac{3 \mathrm{~V}}{30 \Omega}{/tex} = 0.1 A

questions.question_text: A small coil of radius r is placed at the centre of a large coil of radius R, where R >> r. The two coils are coplanar. The mutual induction of the coils is proportional to
questions.question_html: A small coil of radius r is placed at the centre of a large coil of radius R, where R >> r. The two coils are coplanar. The mutual induction of the coils is proportional to
questions.explanation: Magnetic field at the centre of a large coil, {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}{/tex} as, r << R Magnetic flux linkage, {tex}\phi=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R} \times \pi r^{2}{/tex} Thus, {tex}M=\frac{\phi}{I}=\frac{\mu_{0} \pi r^{2}}{2 R}{/tex} {tex}\therefore M \propto \frac{r^{2}}{R}{/tex}

questions.question_text: A coil has resistance 30 ohm and inductive reactance 20 ohm at 50 Hz frequency. If an ac source of 200 volt, 100 Hz, is connected across the coil, the current in the coil will be
questions.question_html: A coil has resistance 30 ohm and inductive reactance 20 ohm at 50 Hz frequency. If an ac source of 200 volt, 100 Hz, is connected across the coil, the current in the coil will be
questions.explanation: {tex}X_{L}=2 \pi f L=20 \Omega{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}L=\frac{20}{2 \pi \times 50}=\frac{20}{100 \pi} \mathrm{H}{/tex} {tex}X_{L}^{\prime}=2 \pi f^{\prime} L=2 \pi \times 100 \times \frac{20}{100 \pi}=40 \Omega{/tex} {tex}Z^{\prime}=\sqrt{R^{2}+X_{L}^{2}}=\sqrt{900+1600}=50 \Omega{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{Z^{\prime}}=\frac{200}{50}{/tex} = 4 A

questions.question_text: Two positive charges
questions.question_html: Two positive charges

options.text: attract each other at times and repel at other times
options.html: attract each other at times and repel at other times

questions.explanation: According to Coulomb's law, unlike charges always attract each other while like charges always repel each other. So two positive charges repel each other.

questions.question_text: A large solid sphere with uniformly distributed positive charge has a smooth narrow tunnel through its centre. A small particle with a negative charge, initially at rest far from the sphere, approaches it along the line of the tunnel, reaches its surface with a speed v, and passes through the tunnel. Its speed at the centre of the sphere will be
questions.question_html: A large solid sphere with uniformly distributed positive charge has a smooth narrow tunnel through its centre. A small particle with a negative charge, initially at rest far from the sphere, approaches it along the line of the tunnel, reaches its surface with a speed v, and passes through the tunnel. Its speed at the centre of the sphere will be
questions.explanation: At infinity, {tex}V_{\infty}=0{/tex} At the surface of the sphere, {tex}V_{s}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{Q}{R}{/tex} At the centre of the sphere, {tex}V_{c}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{3 Q}{2 R}{/tex} Let m be the mass and -q be the charge of the small particle. Let V 0 be speed of the particle at the centre of the sphere. {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=-q\left[V_{\infty}-V_{s}\right]=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q Q}{R}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v_{0}^{2}=-q\left[V_{\infty}-V_{c}\right]=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{3 q Q}{2 R}{/tex} ...(ii) Dividing (ii) by (i), we get {tex}\frac{v_{0}^{2}}{v^{2}}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} = 1.5 {tex}\therefore v_{0}=\sqrt{1.5} v{/tex}

questions.question_text: A charged particle travels along a straight line with a speed v in a region where both electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} and magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} are present. It follows that:
questions.question_html: A charged particle travels along a straight line with a speed v in a region where both electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} and magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} are present. It follows that:

options.text: {tex}|\vec{E}|=v|\vec{B}|{/tex} and the two fields are parallel
{tex}|\vec{E}|=v|\vec{B}|{/tex} and the two fields are perpendicular
{tex}|\vec{B}|= v | \vec{E}|{/tex} and the two fields are perpendicular
options.html: {tex}|\vec{B}|= v | \vec{E}|{/tex} and the two fields are perpendicular

options.text: {tex}|\vec{B}|=v |\vec{E}|{/tex} and the two fields are parallel
options.html: {tex}|\vec{B}|=v |\vec{E}|{/tex} and the two fields are parallel

questions.correct_option_text: {tex}|\vec{E}|=v|\vec{B}|{/tex} and the two fields are perpendicular
questions.explanation: When {tex}\vec{E} \perp \vec{B}{/tex} , qE = qvB {tex}\Rightarrow \ v=\frac{E}{B}=\frac{|\vec{E}|}{|\vec{B}|}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \ |\vec{E}|=v|\vec{B}|{/tex}

questions.question_text: A series circuit consists of an ac source of variable frequency, a 115.0 {tex}\Omega {/tex} resistor, a 1.25 {tex}\mu {\rm{F}}{/tex} capacitor, and a 4.50-mH inductor. The impedance of this circuit when the angular frequency of the ac source is adjusted to half the resonant angular frequency is
questions.question_html: A series circuit consists of an ac source of variable frequency, a 115.0 {tex}\Omega {/tex} resistor, a 1.25 {tex}\mu {\rm{F}}{/tex} capacitor, and a 4.50-mH inductor. The impedance of this circuit when the angular frequency of the ac source is adjusted to half the resonant angular frequency is
questions.explanation: {tex}R = 115\Omega {/tex} {tex}C = 1.25\mu F = 1.25 \times {10^{ - 6}}F{/tex} {tex}L = 4.5mH = 4.5 \times {10^{ - 3}}H{/tex} Resonant angular frequency is given by , {tex}{\omega _0} = {1 \over {\sqrt {LC} }} = {1 \over {\sqrt {4.5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}} \times 1.25 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}} }} = {1 \over {7.5 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}}{/tex} Given that the angular frequency of the ac source, {tex}\omega = {{{\omega _0}} \over 2} = {1 \over {15 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}} = 6666.6rad/s{/tex} Thus, Impedance is given by , {tex}Z = \sqrt {{R^2} + {{\left( {\frac{1}{{\omega C}} - \omega L} \right)}^2}} = \sqrt {{{115}^2} + {{\left[ {\left( {\frac{1}{{6666.6 \times 1.25 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}} \right) - \left( {6666.6 \times 4.5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} \right)} \right]}^2}} {/tex} {tex}Z = 146\Omega {/tex}

questions.question_text: A bar magnet of magnetic moment M, is placed in a magnetic field B. The torque exerted on it is:
questions.question_html: A bar magnet of magnetic moment M, is placed in a magnetic field B. The torque exerted on it is:
questions.explanation: Since torque is cross product of magnetic moment vector and magnetic field, hence it is given by M {tex}\times{/tex} B

questions.question_text: A battery consists of a variable number n of identical cells (having internal resistance r each) which are connected in series. The terminals of the battery are short-circuited and the current I is measured. Which of the graphs shows the correct relationship between I and n?
questions.question_html: A battery consists of a variable number n of identical cells (having internal resistance r each) which are connected in series. The terminals of the battery are short-circuited and the current I is measured. Which of the graphs shows the correct relationship between I and n?
questions.explanation: I = {tex}\frac{n \varepsilon}{n r}=\frac{\varepsilon}{r}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} I is independent of n and it is constant.

questions.question_text: A charge q is located at the centre of a cube. The electric flux through any face is
questions.question_html: A charge q is located at the centre of a cube. The electric flux through any face is
questions.explanation: {tex}\phi_{E}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{1}{6} \frac{4 \pi q}{\left(4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}\right)}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Choose the correct statement. The capacitor can conduct in a d.c. circuit but not an inductor. In d.c. circuit the inductor can conduct but not a capacitor. in d.c. circuit both the inductor and capacitor cannot conduct. The inductor has infinite resistance in a d.c. circuit.
questions.question_html:
Choose the correct statement.

  1. The capacitor can conduct in a d.c. circuit but not an inductor.
  2. In d.c. circuit the inductor can conduct but not a capacitor.
  3. in d.c. circuit both the inductor and capacitor cannot conduct.
  4. The inductor has infinite resistance in a d.c. circuit.
questions.explanation: In a d.c. circuit, an inductor can conduct but not a capacitor. An inductor offers zero resistance to d.c.

questions.question_text: When a current in a coil changes, it induces a back emf in the same coil. The self-induced emf is given by
questions.question_html: When a current in a coil changes, it induces a back emf in the same coil. The self-induced emf is given by
questions.explanation: {tex}N\phi \propto i{/tex} {tex}N\phi = Li{/tex} {tex}L = \frac{{N\phi }}{i}{/tex} {tex}e = - N\frac{{d\phi }}{{dt}} = - \frac{{d(N\phi )}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}e = - L\frac{{di}}{{dt}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: A current is flowing in north direction along a power line. The direction of the magnetic field above it neglecting the earth's field is towards
questions.question_html: A current is flowing in north direction along a power line. The direction of the magnetic field above it neglecting the earth's field is towards
questions.explanation: According to Right Hand Rule, if one points thumb of his right hand in the direction of current, then the direction in which the fingure curls gives the direction of magnetic field at that point. Hence the direction of magnetic field above the wire is east.

questions.question_text: What is magnetic field in the empty space enclosed by toroidal solenoid of radius R?
questions.question_html: What is magnetic field in the empty space enclosed by toroidal solenoid of radius R?
questions.explanation: The magnetic field of a toroid is limited in the annular region of the toroid. It is zero in the empty region enclosed by it and also outside it.

questions.question_text: A magnetic field:
questions.question_html: A magnetic field:

options.text: always exerts a force on a charged particle
options.html: always exerts a force on a charged particle

options.text: never exerts a force on charged particle
options.html: never exerts a force on charged particle

options.text: exerts a force, if the charged particle is moving along the magnetic field line
options.html: exerts a force, if the charged particle is moving along the magnetic field line

options.text: exerts a force, if the charged particle is moving across the magnetic field line
options.html: exerts a force, if the charged particle is moving across the magnetic field line

questions.correct_option_text: exerts a force, if the charged particle is moving across the magnetic field line
questions.explanation: A magnetic field exerts a force on a charged particle if {tex}F=q v B \sin \theta{/tex} is non-zero.

questions.question_text: Which can be the unit of Resistivity from the following?
questions.question_html: Which can be the unit of Resistivity from the following?
questions.explanation: Electrical resistivity of the material of a conductor is the resistance offered by the conductor of length 1 m and area of cross-section 1m 2 . A low resistivity indicates a material that readily allows the movement of electric charge. {tex}\because {/tex} Resistance, {tex}R = \rho \frac{L}{A}{/tex} Where {tex}\rho {/tex} is resistivity, L is the length and A is area. {tex} \Rightarrow \rho = R\frac{A}{L}{/tex} Since unit of R = Ohm, A = (meter) 2 , l = (meter) Unit of resistivity , {tex}\rho = \frac{(( ohm×( metre)^2)}{(metre)}{/tex} {tex}\rho= ohm-metre (or \ Ω- m){/tex}

questions.question_text: The electric field required to keep a water drop of mass m just to remain suspended, when charged with one electron, is
questions.question_html: The electric field required to keep a water drop of mass m just to remain suspended, when charged with one electron, is
questions.explanation: Force exerted by electric field = Weight of water drop eE = mg or E = {tex}\frac{m g}{e}{/tex}

questions.question_text: A 50 Hz a.c. source of 20 V is connected across R and C as shown in the figure. The voltage across R is 12 V. The voltage across C is
questions.question_html: A 50 Hz a.c. source of 20 V is connected across R and C as shown in the figure. The voltage across R is 12 V. The voltage across C is

options.text: not possible to determine, unless values of R and C are given
options.html: not possible to determine, unless values of R and C are given

questions.explanation: For an RC-circuit, {tex}V_{e f f}=\sqrt{V_{R}^{2}+V_{C}^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}V_{C}=\sqrt{V_{e f f}^{2}-V_{R}^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{20^{2}-12^{2}}{/tex} = 16 V

questions.question_text: The induction coil works on the principle of
questions.question_html: The induction coil works on the principle of
questions.explanation: The induction coil works on the principle of mutual induction.

questions.question_text: According to the dipole analogy {tex}{\rm{1}}/{\varepsilon _0}{/tex} corresponds to
questions.question_html: According to the dipole analogy {tex}{\rm{1}}/{\varepsilon _0}{/tex} corresponds to
questions.explanation: {tex}{\mu _0}{/tex} in magnetisim is analogous to {tex}{\rm{1}}/{\varepsilon _0}{/tex} in electricity.

questions.question_text: A wire loop is rotated in a magnetic field. The frequency of change of direction of the induced emf is:
questions.question_html: A wire loop is rotated in a magnetic field. The frequency of change of direction of the induced emf is:
questions.explanation: The direction of induced emf changes after every half revolution i.e., twice per revolution.

questions.question_text: According to Kirchhoff’s Junction Rule,
questions.question_html: According to Kirchhoff’s Junction Rule,

options.text: At any junction of circuit elements, the sum of currents entering and leaving the junction must be negative.
options.html: At any junction of circuit elements, the sum of currents entering and leaving the junction must be negative.

options.text: At any junction of circuit elements, the sum of currents entering the junction must equal the sum of currents leaving it.
options.html: At any junction of circuit elements, the sum of currents entering the junction must equal the sum of currents leaving it.

options.text: At any junction of circuit elements, the sum of currents entering and leaving the junction must be positive.
options.html: At any junction of circuit elements, the sum of currents entering and leaving the junction must be positive.

options.text: At any junction of circuit elements, the sum of currents entering the junction must not equal the sum of currents leaving it.
options.html: At any junction of circuit elements, the sum of currents entering the junction must not equal the sum of currents leaving it.

questions.correct_option_text: At any junction of circuit elements, the sum of currents entering the junction must equal the sum of currents leaving it.
questions.explanation: Kirchhoff’s junction rule is based on the principle of conservation of charge. For steady currents, there can be no accumulation of charge in a junction. The currents entering a junction bring charge to the junction and the currents leaving a junction carry away the charge brought to the junction.

questions.question_text: The instrument for the accurate measurement of the e.m.f of a cell is
questions.question_html: The instrument for the accurate measurement of the e.m.f of a cell is
questions.explanation: Both potentiometer and voltmeter are devices to measure potential difference. Emf is the terminal p.d between the electrodes of a cell in open circuit, i.e., when no current is drawn from it. Potentiometer measures the potential difference using null deflection method, where no current is drawn from the cell; whereas voltmeter needs a small current to show deflection. So, accurate measurement of p.d is done using a potentiometer.

questions.question_text: A 1250 W heater operates at 115 V. What is the resistance of the heating coil?
questions.question_html: A 1250 W heater operates at 115 V. What is the resistance of the heating coil?
questions.explanation: R = {tex}\frac{V^{2}}{P}=\frac{115 \times 115}{1250}{/tex} = 10.6 {tex}\Omega{/tex}

questions.question_text: In an LCR-series a.c. circuit, the voltage across each of the components. L, C and R is 50 V. The voltage across the LC-combination will be:
questions.question_html: In an LCR-series a.c. circuit, the voltage across each of the components. L, C and R is 50 V. The voltage across the LC-combination will be:
questions.explanation: The voltage across L and C are out of phase with each other. Hence, the voltage across LC-combination is zero.

questions.question_text: Assume that a motor in which the coils have a total resistance of {tex}{\rm{1}}0{\rm{ }}\Omega {/tex} is supplied by a voltage of 120 V. When the motor is running at its maximum speed, the back emf is 70 V. Current in the coils when the motor is turned on and when it has reached maximum speed are
questions.question_html: Assume that a motor in which the coils have a total resistance of {tex}{\rm{1}}0{\rm{ }}\Omega {/tex} is supplied by a voltage of 120 V. When the motor is running at its maximum speed, the back emf is 70 V. Current in the coils when the motor is turned on and when it has reached maximum speed are
questions.explanation: When motor is turned on {tex}i = {V \over R} = {{120} \over {10}} = 12A{/tex} When it has reached maximum speed, the back emf is 70 V, then current will be {tex}i = {V \over R} = {{120 - 70} \over {10}} = 5A{/tex}

questions.question_text: In which case of comparing solenoid and bar magnet there is no exact similarity?
questions.question_html: In which case of comparing solenoid and bar magnet there is no exact similarity?

options.text: solenoid can be broken into two weaker solenoids
options.html: solenoid can be broken into two weaker solenoids

options.text: moving a small compass needle in the neighbourhood of a solenoid enables tracing the flux lines
options.html: moving a small compass needle in the neighbourhood of a solenoid enables tracing the flux lines

options.text: flux lines enter one end of a solenoid
options.html: flux lines enter one end of a solenoid

options.text: there is a current entering and a current leaving a solenoid
options.html: there is a current entering and a current leaving a solenoid

questions.correct_option_text: there is a current entering and a current leaving a solenoid
questions.explanation: The solenoid will change the field direction and the magnetic pole polarity as current direction is changed. But, the bar magnet will always have same poles, and it does not change.

questions.question_text: In the inductive circuit given in the figure, the current rises after the switch is closed. At the instant, when the current is 25 mA, then the potential difference across the inductor will be:
questions.question_html: In the inductive circuit given in the figure, the current rises after the switch is closed. At the instant, when the current is 25 mA, then the potential difference across the inductor will be:
questions.explanation: {tex}V_{R}=R I=4000 \times 25 \times 10^{-3}=100 \mathrm{~V}{/tex} {tex}V_{L}=\sqrt{V_{\text {eff }}^{2}-V_{R}^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{260^{2}-100^{2}}=240 \mathrm{~V}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Three resistances P, Q, R each of 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and an unknown resistance S form the four arms of a Wheatstone's bridge circuit. When the resistance of 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is connected in parallel to S, the bridge gets balanced. What is the value of S?
questions.question_html: Three resistances P, Q, R each of 2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and an unknown resistance S form the four arms of a Wheatstone's bridge circuit. When the resistance of 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} is connected in parallel to S, the bridge gets balanced. What is the value of S?
questions.explanation: The fourth arm has resistances S and 6 {tex}\Omega{/tex} in parallel with equivalent resistance = {tex}\frac{6 S}{6 \ + \ S} \Omega{/tex} For the balanced Wheatstone bridge, {tex}\frac{P}{Q}=\frac{R}{6 S}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{2}{2}=\frac{2(6 \ + \ S)}{6 S}{/tex} or 3S = 6 + S or S = 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex}

questions.question_text: You are given n resistors, each of resistance R. These are combined first to get the minimum possible resistance, then these are combined to get the maximum possible resistance. The ratio between minimum to maximum resistance is
questions.question_html: You are given n resistors, each of resistance R. These are combined first to get the minimum possible resistance, then these are combined to get the maximum possible resistance. The ratio between minimum to maximum resistance is
questions.explanation: For minimum resultant, the n resistors are connected in parallel. R min. = {tex}\frac{r}{n}{/tex} For maximum resultant, the n resistors are connected in series. R max. = nr then their ratio becomes ; {tex}\frac{R_{\min }}{R_{\max }}=\frac{r}{n} \times \frac{1}{n r}=\frac{1}{n^{2}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: The electric field due to a uniformly charged sphere of radius R as a function of the distance from its centre is represented graphically by:
questions.question_html: The electric field due to a uniformly charged sphere of radius R as a function of the distance from its centre is represented graphically by:
questions.explanation: For points inside a charged sphere, E {tex}\propto{/tex} r And for points outside a charged sphere, E {tex}\propto \frac{1}{r^{2}}{/tex} Hence this option is correct.

questions.question_text: The work done in rotating a magnet of magnetic moment 2 Am 2 in a magnetic field of {tex}{\rm{5 }} \times {\rm{ 1}}{0^{ - {\rm{3}}}}{/tex} T form the direction along the magnetic field to opposite direction to the magnetic field, is
questions.question_html: The work done in rotating a magnet of magnetic moment 2 Am2 in a magnetic field of {tex}{\rm{5 }} \times {\rm{ 1}}{0^{ - {\rm{3}}}}{/tex} T form the direction along the magnetic field to opposite direction to the magnetic field, is
questions.explanation: magnetic moment (m)= 2Am 2 and magnetic field (B)is =5 x 10 -3 T The potential energy of a magnetic dipole of moment m placed in a magnetic field is {tex}U = - mB\cos \theta{/tex} When the magnet is aligned in the direction of the field, and the initial potential energy U i = -mB When the magnet is aligned opposite to the direction of the field {tex}\theta = 180{/tex} , its potential energy is U f = mB Work done in rotating the magnet is equal to the change in its potential energy. W = U f - U i = mB - (-mB) = {tex}2mB = 2 \times 2 \times 5 \times {10^{ - 3}}{/tex} {tex} = 2 \times {10^{ - 2}}J{/tex}

questions.question_text: Needles N 1 , N 2 and N 3 are made of a ferromagnetic, a paramagnetic and a diamagnetic substance respectively. A magnet, when brought close to them, will
questions.question_html: Needles N1, N2 and N3 are made of a ferromagnetic, a paramagnetic and a diamagnetic substance respectively. A magnet, when brought close to them, will

options.text: attract N 1 strongly, N 2 weakly and repel N 3 weakly
options.html: attract N<sub>1</sub> strongly, N<sub>2</sub> weakly and repel N<sub>3</sub> weakly

options.text: attract N 1 strongly, but repel N 2 and N 3 weakly
options.html: attract N<sub>1</sub> strongly, but repel N<sub>2</sub> and N<sub>3</sub> weakly

options.text: attract N 1 and N 2 strongly but repel N 3
options.html: attract N<sub>1</sub> and N<sub>2</sub> strongly but repel N<sub>3</sub>

questions.correct_option_text: attract N 1 strongly, N 2 weakly and repel N 3 weakly
questions.explanation: A magnet attracts ferromagnetic substance strongly, a paramagnetic substance weakly and a diamagnetic substance is repelled by it.

questions.question_text: Assertion (A): If the distance between parallel plates of a capacitor is halved and dielectric constant is three times, then the capacitor becomes 6 times. Reason (R): Capacity of a capacitor does not depend upon the nature of the material.
questions.question_html:
Assertion (A): If the distance between parallel plates of a capacitor is halved and dielectric constant is three times, then the capacitor becomes 6 times.
Reason (R): Capacity of a capacitor does not depend upon the nature of the material.

options.text: A is true but R is false.
options.html: A is true but R is false.

options.text: A is false but R is true.

questions.question_text: Assertion (A): The sensitivity of a moving coil galvanometer is increased by placing a suitable magnetic material as a core inside the coil. Reason (R): Soft iron has high magnetic permeability and cannot be easily magnetized or demagnetized.
questions.question_html:
Assertion (A): The sensitivity of a moving coil galvanometer is increased by placing a suitable magnetic material as a core inside the coil.
Reason (R): Soft iron has high magnetic permeability and cannot be easily magnetized or demagnetized.

options.html: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.

questions.correct_option_text: A is true but R is false.
questions.explanation: Sensitivity of a moving coil galvanometer, = {tex}\frac{\theta}{\mathrm{I}}=\frac{N B A}{C}{/tex} If B increases, by using iron core {tex}\frac{\theta}{\mathrm{I}}{/tex} i.e.; sensitivity increases. Soft iron can be easily magnetised and de magnetized.

Reason (R): The presence of a large magnetic flux will maintain an induced current in the coil.

questions.question_text: Assertion (A): The surface densities of two spherical conductors of different radii are equal. Then the electric field intensities near their surface are also equal. Reason (R): Surface density is equal to charge per unit area.
questions.question_html:

Reason (R): Surface density is equal to charge per unit area.

questions.correct_option_text: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
questions.explanation: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A. As, {tex}\sigma_{1}=\sigma_{2}{/tex} (given) {tex}\therefore \frac{q_{1 }}{4 \pi r_{1}^{2}}=\frac{q_{2}}{4 \pi r_{2}^{2}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{q_{1}}{q_{2}}=\frac{r_{1}^{2}}{r_{2}^{2}}{/tex} [Let r 1 and r 2 be two different radii.] Then the ratio of electric field intensifies near the surface of spherical conductors, {tex}\frac{E_{1}}{E_{2}}=\frac{q_{1}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r_{1}^{2}} \times \frac{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r_{2}^{2}}{q_{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{q_{1}}{q_{2}} \times \frac{r_{2}^{2}}{r_{1}^{2}}=\frac{q_{1}}{q_{2}} \times \frac{q_{2}}{q_{1}}{/tex} = 1 i.e., E 1 = E 2

questions.question_text: A semi-circular arc of radius 'a' is charged uniformly and the charge per unit lengths is {tex}\lambda{/tex} . The electric field at the centre is:
questions.question_html: A semi-circular arc of radius 'a' is charged uniformly and the charge per unit lengths is {tex}\lambda{/tex}. The electric field at the centre is:
questions.explanation: {tex}\lambda{/tex} = linear charge density; Charge on elementary portions is given by dq = {tex}\lambda{/tex} dx Electric field at O is given by , {tex}d E=\frac{\lambda d x}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} a^{2}}{/tex} Horizontal electric field, i.e., perpendicular to AO, will cancelled. Hence, net electric field = addition of all electrical fields in direction of AO {tex}=\sum \mathrm{dE} \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow E=\int \frac{\lambda d x}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} a^{2}} \cos \theta{/tex} Also, {tex}\mathrm{d} \theta=\frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} or {tex}\mathrm{dx}=\mathrm{ad} \theta{/tex} {tex}E=\int_{-\pi / 2}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\lambda \cos \theta d \theta}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} a}=\frac{\lambda}{4\pi \varepsilon_{0} a}[\sin \theta]_{-\pi / 2}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\lambda}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} a}[1-(-1)]=\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_{0} a}{/tex}

questions.question_text: A charge Q is enclosed by a Gaussian spherical surface of radius R. If the radius is doubled, then the outward electric flux will
questions.question_html: A charge Q is enclosed by a Gaussian spherical surface of radius R. If the radius is doubled, then the outward electric flux will
questions.explanation: As the charge Q enclosed by the Gaussian surface remains the same, so {tex}\phi=\frac {q}{ \varepsilon_{0^{\prime}}}{/tex} will also remain the same.

questions.question_text: Instructions for Question 52 to 55: Capacitance is the ratio of the change in the electric charge of a system to the corresponding change in its electric potential. Capacitors consist of two parallel conductive plates (usually a metal) which are prevented from touching each other (separated) by an insulating material called the dielectric . When a voltage is applied to these plates an electrical current flows charging up one plate with a positive charge with respect to the supply voltage and the other plate with an equal and opposite negative charge. The generalized equation for the capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor is given as: C = k {tex}ϵ_0\frac Ad{/tex} where {tex}\varepsilon_0{/tex} represents the absolute permittivity of the dielectric material being used, and the charge is given by q = CV When a conductor is placed in an electric field, the field inside the conductor is:-
questions.question_html:
Instructions for Question 52 to 55:

Capacitance is the ratio of the change in the electric charge of a system to the corresponding change in its electric potential. Capacitors consist of two parallel conductive plates (usually a metal) which are prevented from touching each other (separated) by an insulating material called the dielectric. When a voltage is applied to these plates an electrical current flows charging up one plate with a positive charge with respect to the supply voltage and the other plate with an equal and opposite negative charge. The generalized equation for the capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor is given as: C = k{tex}ϵ_0\frac Ad{/tex}  where {tex}\varepsilon_0{/tex} represents the absolute permittivity of the dielectric material being used, and the charge is given by q = CV 


When a conductor is placed in an electric field, the field inside the conductor is:-
questions.explanation: zero

Instructions for Question 52 to 55:

Capacitance is the ratio of the change in the electric charge of a system to the corresponding change in its electric potential. Capacitors consist of two parallel conductive plates (usually a metal) which are prevented from touching each other (separated) by an insulating material called the dielectric. When a voltage is applied to these plates an electrical current flows charging up one plate with a positive charge with respect to the supply voltage and the other plate with an equal and opposite negative charge. The generalized equation for the capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor is given as: C = k{tex}ϵ_0\frac Ad{/tex}  where {tex}\varepsilon_0{/tex} represents the absolute permittivity of the dielectric material being used, and the charge is given by q = CV 


Why does the capacitor block dc signal at a steady-state?

options.text: Due to the zero frequency of dc signal

questions.explanation: Due to zero frequency of dc signal

questions.question_text: Instructions for Question 52 to 55: Capacitance is the ratio of the change in the electric charge of a system to the corresponding change in its electric potential. Capacitors consist of two parallel conductive plates (usually a metal) which are prevented from touching each other (separated) by an insulating material called the dielectric . When a voltage is applied to these plates an electrical current flows charging up one plate with a positive charge with respect to the supply voltage and the other plate with an equal and opposite negative charge. The generalized equation for the capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor is given as: C = k {tex}ϵ_0\frac Ad{/tex} where {tex}\varepsilon_0{/tex} represents the absolute permittivity of the dielectric material being used, and the charge is given by q = CV What is the value of capacitance of a capacitor which has a voltage of 4V and has 16C of charge?
questions.question_html:
Instructions for Question 52 to 55:

Capacitance is the ratio of the change in the electric charge of a system to the corresponding change in its electric potential. Capacitors consist of two parallel conductive plates (usually a metal) which are prevented from touching each other (separated) by an insulating material called the dielectric. When a voltage is applied to these plates an electrical current flows charging up one plate with a positive charge with respect to the supply voltage and the other plate with an equal and opposite negative charge. The generalized equation for the capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor is given as: C = k{tex}ϵ_0\frac Ad{/tex}  where {tex}\varepsilon_0{/tex} represents the absolute permittivity of the dielectric material being used, and the charge is given by q = CV 


What is the value of capacitance of a capacitor which has a voltage of 4V and has 16C of charge?
questions.explanation: 4F
